agent1_snippets.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Deterrence and Patient Education
agent1_snippets.content: Telehealth or telephone check-ins, smartphone reminders, delivery services, directly observed therapy, and pillboxes may enhance adherence. Switching to a different formulation or regime for oral PrEP (eg, moving to on-demand) or switching to long-acting intramuscular cabotegravir may also improve adherence. Seeking further financial support may improve adherence for those who do not have insurance and cannot afford the medications. For patients with substance use disorders, referral for counseling or a treatment program may be successful. Options should be tailored to fit the client's preferences and life situation. All measures must be supportive, recognizing the challenges in maintaining long-term adherence to therapy and the socioeconomic context in which the client lives.

agent1_snippets.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Preexposure prophylaxis of HIV (PrEP) is an essential component of the recommended package for HIV prevention, according to the World Health Organization (WHO) and United States Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). [3] [4] Preexposure prophylaxis (PrEP) involves the intake of antiretroviral medications either continuously or for identified high-risk events and is highly effective in preventing HIV. Compared to placebo, consistent use of daily oral PrEP reduces the risk of sexual transmission of HIV by up to 92%. [5] PrEP with bimonthly injectable cabotegravir lowers the chance of sexual transmission of HIV by about 70% compared to those taking tenofovir disiproxil fumarate/emtricitabine (TDF/FTC). [6] [CDC. PrEP Guidelines. 2021] Though one trial of PWID observed individuals 40 years or older, those who used methamphetamines, and those with recent incarcerations had lower adherence than those without these risk factors, other studies have indicated the efficacy and cost-effectiveness of PrEP in preventing HIV in this population. [7] [8]

agent1_snippets.content: Patients receiving cabotegravir injections must be advised that if they discontinue medication, the gradually declining levels of cabotegravir raise the risk of developing a drug-resistant strain if HIV infection occurs at that time.[CDC. PrEP Guidelines. 2021] Therefore, individuals need to keep regular injection appointments.

agent1_snippets.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Complications -- Adverse Events
agent1_snippets.content: Long-acting cabotegravir and TDF/FTC likewise demonstrated similar risks of serious adverse events. There were no differences between long-acting cabotegravir and TDF/FTC in serious kidney or liver adverse events, discontinuation of therapy due to liver events, or sexually transmitted infections. [35] [64] Injection site reactions such as pain, tenderness, and induration were reported in 81.4% of patients receiving cabotegravir. Reactions were generally mild, disappeared within a few days, and occurred most frequently with the first 2 to 3 injections. These reactions can be managed with over-the-counter pain medications and hot compresses for 15 to 20 minutes after the injection. [30]

For example, due to its ease of use for those facing barriers to regular, daily medication use, cabotegravir may be ideal for PrEP. agent1_snippets.content: This is particularly true for new PrEP technologies. In the United States, the initial pricing for long-acting cabotegravir was $22,000 per year. Under a voluntary licensing agreement, about 90 countries across Africa (except Libya) and other low- and lower-middle-income countries can purchase less expensive generic versions of cabotegravir.[UNAIDS. Global Report. 2023] However, generic production will take years to scale up, and the costs may remain prohibitively high for impoverished people. Upper- and middle-income regions such as Latin America, where the incidence of HIV is increasing, are not included in the deal.[UNAIDS. agent1_snippets.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Etiology
agent1_snippets.content: The use of antiretrovirals as PrEP is a less expensive, more straightforward, and more effective way to prevent HIV in higher-risk populations compared to treatment with antiretroviral therapy. Results from multiple large trials demonstrated the efficacy of oral PrEP in decreasing HIV transmission risk in people at high risk of acquiring HIV across sex, age, risk category, and study duration. [30] [31] [32] [33] The risk of HIV is up to 92% lower for people who take oral PrEP consistently compared to those who take a placebo. The effectiveness of PrEP is significantly reduced if used inconsistently.[CDC. PrEP HIV Prevention in the US. 2021] The first long-acting injectable PrEP, cabotegravir, was approved in the United States in 2021. Men, women, and transgender women in the cabotegravir groups of 2 large studies had a 69% to 90% lower chance of contracting HIV through sexual transmission than those taking tenofovir disoproxil fumarate/emtricitabine. [34] [35] [36] [CDC. PrEP HIV Prevention in the US. 2021]

agent1_snippets.title: HIV and AIDS -- Treatment / Management -- Recommended Therapy for Patients on Preexposure Prophylaxis
agent1_snippets.content: Patients on preexposure prophylaxis with tenofovir alafenamide or tenofovir disoproxil fumarate with emtricitabine who subsequently acquire HIV require resistance testing before initiating therapy. They can be initiated on INSTI-containing regimens outlined above while genotype or resistance testing is pending. Patients who acquire HIV after receiving cabotegravir for preexposure prophylaxis require INSTI genotyping before beginning therapy with an INSTI-based regimen. Although the results are pending, a boosted protease inhibitor regimen containing darunavir and a tenofovir-based dual NRTI coformulation should be used. Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention ," for more information. [24]

agent1_snippets.title: HIV-2 Infection -- Deterrence and Patient Education
agent1_snippets.content: Pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) should be offered to patients at substantial risk of acquiring HIV infection. Although there are no data explicitly addressing PrEP in preventing HIV-2, the antiretrovirals used for PrEP are active against both HIV-1 and HIV-2, and similarly, high levels of efficacy would be expected. [58] Currently, less is known about the potential effectiveness of long-acting injectable PrEP in the prevention of HIV-2, though recent in vitro studies results have shown cabotegravir to have efficacy against HIV-2. [59]

agent1_snippets.title: Sexually Transmitted Infections -- Deterrence and Patient Education
agent1_snippets.content: Of the 1.2 million individuals in the US who are considered at high risk for HIV, where PrEP is recommended, only about 25% are taking the therapy. Current recommended PrEP treatments include: Oral emtricitabine with tenofovir disoproxil fumarate is FDA-approved for PrEP. Oral emtricitabine with tenofovir alafenamide is FDA-approved for PrEP except for people born as women who engage in vaginal intercourse, as this group has not been adequately studied. Injectable cabotegravir is given every 2 months after the first 2 monthly administrations.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2032
agent2_snippets.content: Cabotegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class identified as Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use. As integrase inhibitors, each of these agents targets the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and serving as essential components of systemic antiretroviral therapy. Their shared classification underscores a common mechanism of action and therapeutic purpose in the treatment of HIV infection, highlighting the importance of this drug class in modern HIV management.

Their inclusion in this class highlights a shared mechanism of action and therapeutic role in antiretroviral regimens, making them important options for clinicians managing patients with HIV.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 650
agent2_snippets.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, which represents a major route of transmission. The use of methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in patients with HIV infection, highlighting the need for careful medication selection in this population. Several antiretroviral agents are associated with the prevention or treatment of HIV infection. Delavirdin‑mesylate and dolutegravir have been reported to have preventive activity against HIV acquisition, and raltegravir is also recognized for its ability to prevent HIV infection. Cabotegravir, in contrast, is used primarily for the treatment of established HIV infection. Dolutegravir, cabotegravir, and raltegravir all belong to the class of integrase inhibitors, a group of systematic anti‑infective agents that target the viral integrase enzyme to block viral replication. Understanding these relationships assists clinicians in selecting appropriate preventive strategies, therapeutic regimens, and avoiding contraindicated drugs for individuals living with or at risk for HIV.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 135
agent2_snippets.content: HIV infection is strongly associated with shared needle use, which constitutes a major transmission route, and its prevention and treatment rely on antiretroviral agents classified by mechanism of action. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, mimic natural nucleosides, and cause chain termination, thereby inhibiting viral DNA synthesis. Key NRTIs include tenofovir disoproxil, abacavir, didanosine (dideoxycytidine), emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, and zidovudine; each has demonstrated activity in pre‑exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) and in early or combination treatment regimens to suppress viral replication. The non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor delavirdine mesylate also possesses preventive properties by directly inhibiting reverse transcriptase. Integrase inhibitors—cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir—block the viral integrase enzyme, preventing integration of viral DNA into the host genome; cabotegravir is notable for its dual use in both treatment of established infection and PrEP, while the other agents are primarily employed for treatment but may be used preventively in high‑risk settings. Collectively, the correct classification and combined use of NRTIs, non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors, and integrase inhibitors form the backbone of modern HIV prevention strategies and therapeutic protocols, optimizing patient outcomes through viral suppression and reduced acquisition risk.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 246
agent2_snippets.content: HIV infection is strongly linked to the practice of shared needle use, which constitutes a major route of transmission. A variety of antiretroviral agents can either prevent acquisition of HIV or treat established infection. Among the nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs), abacavir, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine, dideoxycytidine (also known as didanosine), and tenofovir disoproxil are recognized members of this drug class. Each of these NRTIs—abacavir, delavirdin‑mesylate (a non‑NRTI), emtricitabine, lamivudine, raltegravir, stavudine, and zidovudine—has been shown to have preventive activity against HIV infection when used in pre‑exposure prophylaxis or as part of combination regimens. Integrase inhibitors, another major class of antiretroviral drugs used systemically, include cabotegravir, dolutegravir, and raltegravir, all of which are classified as members of the integrase inhibitor group. Cabotegravir, in particular, possesses both preventive potential and therapeutic efficacy, being noted for its ability to treat HIV infection. Dolutegravir also demonstrates preventive properties and belongs to the integrase inhibitor class. Overall, the prevention of HIV infection can be achieved through the use of multiple agents across different drug classes—NRTIs such as abacavir, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine, and tenofovir disoproxil, as well as integrase inhibitors like cabotegravir, dolutegravir, and raltegravir. For patients already infected, cabotegravir offers a treatment option, while the broader spectrum of NRTIs and integrase inhibitors provides the foundation for effective antiretroviral therapy. Understanding the relationships among transmission risk factors, preventive agents, therapeutic drugs, and their class memberships is essential for comprehensive HIV management and patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 73
agent2_snippets.content: Direct‑acting antivirals (DAAs) encompass several pharmacologic classes used to treat viral infections, including HIV; within DAAs, cyclic amines such as rimantadin alter cellular synthetic activity, while integrase inhibitors, non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) and nucleoside/nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) constitute additional subclasses. NRTIs include abacavir (synonymous with dideoxyadenosine), dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine, and tenofovir disoproxil, and are employed both for HIV prevention (e.g., abacavir, emtricitabine, lamivudine) and therapy. NNRTIs comprise delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesylate, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, doravirine, rilpivirine, and raltegravir‑type agents that bind distinct sites on reverse transcriptase; doravirine is contraindicated with rifampicin and rilpivirine with pantoprazole due to drug‑drug interaction risks. Integrase inhibitors—dolutegravir, elvitegravir, raltegravir, cabotegravir, and fostemsavir—block the viral integrase enzyme required for proviral integration. Fixed‑dose combination regimens such as abacavir/dolutegravir/lamivudine and abacavir/lamivudine (also representing dideoxyadenosine/lamivudine) are classified as “Antivirals for treatment of HIV infections, combinations” and exemplify DAA‑based HIV therapy. Antiviral combinations specifically formulated for HIV are themselves a type of DAA. Shared needle use is identified as a direct risk factor for HIV acquisition, whereas methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in patients with HIV infection, underscoring important safety considerations in this population. Together, these entities delineate the classification, mechanisms of action, therapeutic and preventive roles, combination formulations, nomenclature equivalences, and contraindications relevant to comprehensive HIV management.

agent2_snippets.content: HIV infection is strongly associated with shared‑needle use, which constitutes a major route of viral transmission. A broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents has been identified for both prevention (pre‑exposure prophylaxis) and treatment of HIV infection. Preventive activity has been reported for delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesylate, dolutegravir, efavirenz, elvitegravir, emtricitabine, etravirine, lamivudine, nevirapine, raltegravir, rilpivirine, stavudine, zidovudine and tenofovir disoproxil, whereas cabotegravir is highlighted for its therapeutic effect in established infection. Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) – delavirdin, rilpivirine, efavirenz, etravirine and nevirapine – bind directly to the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme and inhibit viral replication. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) – dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine and tenofovir disoproxil – require intracellular phosphorylation and act as chain terminators of reverse transcription. Integrase inhibitors – cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir – target the HIV integrase enzyme, blocking integration of viral DNA into the host genome.
